elements.sent-2411-001: Probabilistic Reachable Set (PRS) plays a crucial role in many fields of autonomous systems, yet efficiently generating PRS remains a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper presents a learning approach to generating 2-dimensional PRS for states in a dynamic system.
elements.sent-2411-003: Traditional methods such as Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis, Monte Carlo, and Gaussian process classification face significant computational challenges or require detailed dynamics information, limiting their applicability in realistic situations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Existing data-driven methods may lack accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-005: To overcome these limitations, we propose leveraging neural networks, commonly used in imitation learning and computer vision, to imitate expert methods to generate PRS approximations.
elements.sent-2411-006: We trained the neural networks using a multi-label, self-supervised learning approach.
elements.sent-2411-007: We selected the fine-tuned convex approximation method as the expert to create expert PRS.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we continued sampling from the distribution to obtain a diverse array of sample sets.
elements.sent-2411-009: Given a small sample set, the trained neural networks can replicate the PRS approximation generated by the expert method, while the generation speed is much faster.
elements.sent-2411-010: Note to Practitioners---Reachability analysis is one of the popular methods for solving real-time motion planning problems for dynamic systems under uncertainty while ensuring safety.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, determining the reachable set is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-012: Traditional methods are computationally expensive and often require extensive system information.
elements.sent-2411-013: Meanwhile, neural networks have been shown to construct distributions effectively.
elements.sent-2411-014: Hence, we propose leveraging the capability of neural networks to model the distribution of reachable sets.
elements.sent-2411-015: To simplify the creation of training datasets, we incorporate self-supervised learning in training the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-016: The proposed method offers several advantages: it is easy to use, generates accurate results quickly, and requires less system information.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, as neural networks are statistical methods relying on data-driven insights, their effectiveness inherently depends on the availability of sufficient historical data for the system.
elements.sent-2411-018: Autonomous system operations face various uncertainties and hazards~\cite{thompson2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The reachable set approach is an important method in collision avoidance~\cite{zhou2015reachable, lin2015collision , safety verification~\cite{el2021online and path planning~\cite{yang2023guaranteed for Autonomous systems.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, finding the deterministic reachable sets with 100\% confidence level is difficult~\cite{althoff2021set .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Meanwhile, the deterministic reachable sets may be too large and over-conservative to be applicable~\cite{chen2022reachability .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Finding the Probabilistic Reachable Set (PRS) is more realistic, incorporating uncertainties and stochastic elements in the system dynamics or initial conditions~\cite{jafarpourprobabilistic .}
elements.sent-2411-023: A brief visualization of PRS is shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig: reachableSet , where PRS with different confidence levels are colored.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figure/Reachable_set.png} \caption{Probabilistic Reachable set represents the set of possible states a dynamical system can reach from an initial state. For instance, a PRS with a 95\% confidence level means that 95\% of the possible system trajectories, considering uncertainties or noise, will remain within the set bounds} \label{Fig: reachableSet}
elements.sent-2411-025: One method for determining reachable sets under bounded uncertainties is the Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach formulates the problem as a partial differential equation that can be addressed using numerical techniques.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although this method is manageable in scenarios with fewer dimensions, its efficiency significantly diminishes as the dimensionality increases~\cite{lew2022safe}\cite{chen2018decomposition .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, HJ reachability analysis requires detailed information about the dynamics or uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-029: Meanwhile, strong assumptions are required, such as the absence of time correlations in parameter uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-030: These limitations make it unsuitable for certain realistic situations~\cite{lew2022safe .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Fortunately, we can identify the reachable sets using a learning-based method given a large dataset from an unknown distribution.
elements.sent-2411-032: Generating a reachable set with machine learning/data-driven has been explored.
elements.sent-2411-033: A trained locally-weighted regression algorithm and nonlinear SVM classifiers can predict the reachability of the two-point boundary value problem (2PBVP)~\cite{allen2014machine .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Other data-driven methods such as the Gauss process~\cite{cao2021estimating , Monte Carlo~\cite{devonport2020data , and stochastic disturbance~\cite{fiacchini2021probabilistic have also been developed to approximate reachable set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, all of those methods have significant limitations in practice for three aspects: a) they all require a lot of samples to perform; b) they cannot provide fast and accurate probabilistic reachable set with adjustable confidence levels; c) their PRS bound is not convex and linear.

elements.sent-2411-046: Therefore, we propose designing a transformer-based neural network to generate PRS, and we further employ self-supervised learning techniques to train this network.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this paper, we propose a method that uses self-supervised and multi-label training to train a transformer-based neural network for generating convex approximations of PRS from a small number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-048: The numerical experiments prove that the neural network can generate reliable convex approximations when the given samples come from a distribution that has been learned by the network.
elements.sent-2411-049: This effective method relies on just one assumption:.
elements.sent-2411-050: Assumption 1: a large number of previous samples have been observed before the predicting task.
elements.sent-2411-051: This assumption is satisfied in many fields such as autonomous driving~\cite{sun2020scalability , flight landing prediction~\cite{navarro2024amelia , and robotics~\cite{gilles2023metagraspnetv2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our method offers three unique advantages that set it apart from other methods:.
elements.sent-2411-053: Advantage 1: No information about the desired system is needed; only a few samples of the desired distribution are required for PRS generating.
elements.sent-2411-054: Advantage 2: The generation speed is extremely fast, making it suitable for online applications.
elements.sent-2411-055: Advantage 3: All the PRS bound generated are convex and linear.
elements.sent-2411-056: Advantage 4: Very few labeled data are needed for training, which simplifies the training process and makes large-scale training feasible.
elements.sent-2411-057: The main goal of the paper is to generate a convex approximation that can capture the PRS of an unknown static uncertainty whose data samples are gradually observed.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this section, we will introduce and discuss the concepts of reachable sets and PRS associated with a dynamic system with uncertain discrete-time characteristics ~\cite{wu2024fine,wu2024data .}

elements.sent-2411-097: The expert method is proposed in~\cite{wu2023efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-098: The expert method can generate the PRS of an uncertain dynamic system.
elements.sent-2411-099: This data-driven method builds on a kernel density estimator (KDE) that can generate the PRS, accelerated by a fast Fourier transform (FFT).
elements.sent-2411-100: These KDE values are then utilized within an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) framework to find a minimal parallelogram box that approximates the probabilistic geofence.
elements.sent-2411-101: This ILP solution effectively encapsulates the area where the state is expected to stay with high confidence.
elements.sent-2411-102: A brief process of the ILP Heuristic Algorithm is shown in the Algorithm~\ref{ag: ILP .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{ILP Heuristic Algorithm}\label{ag: ILP} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Function{BoxApprox}{$r_{\min}, c_{\min}, r_{\max}, c_{\max}, \omega_{ij}$} \State $\omega'_{ij} \gets \omega_{ij}.\text{copy}()$ \State $z_{ij}[1:N][1:N] \gets 0$ \State $r_{\min}, c_{\min} \gets \arg \max_{i,j} \omega'_{ij}$ \State $r_{\max} \gets r_{\min}$, $c_{\max} \gets c_{\min}$ \While{$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \omega_{ij} z_{ij} < \alpha \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \omega_{ij}$} \State $i, j \gets \arg \max_{i,j} \omega'_{ij}$ \State $\omega'_{ij}[i, j] \gets 0.0$ \State $r_{\min} \gets \min(r_{\min}, i)$, $r_{\max} \gets \max(r_{\max}, i)$ \State $c_{\min} \gets \min(c_{\min}, j)$, $c_{\max} \gets \max(c_{\max}, j)$ \State $z_{ij}[r_{\min}:r_{\max}][c_{\min}:c_{\max}] \gets 1$ \EndWhile \State \Return $z_{ij}$ \EndFunction \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-103: The ILP Heuristic Algorithm approximates solutions for integer linear programming problems using heuristics.
elements.sent-2411-104: It starts with a weight matrix $\omega_{ij}$ and a zero matrix $z_{ij}$, setting initial indices based on $\omega_{ij}$'s maximum values.
elements.sent-2411-105: The algorithm loops to update $z_{ij}$ until the product of $z_{ij}$ and $\omega_{ij}$ meets a threshold $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Each iteration zeros out the maximum $\omega_{ij}$ and adjusts boundary indices.
elements.sent-2411-107: The corresponding $z_{ij}$ entry is set to one within these boundaries, yielding the matrix as an efficient approximation of the ILP solution.

elements.sent-2411-129: The neural network generated an output based on the current weights of the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-130: Then the losses between each expert and the output are calculated and the smallest loss will be used to train the neural network.

elements.sent-2411-132: Like many regression machine learning research studies, we use the MSE loss function as shown in Eq.~\ref{eq:loss .}
elements.sent-2411-133: MSE loss can help the neural network minimize the average error between the expert and the output. \begin{equation}\text{Loss} = \min_{j \in [1, 10]}{\frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} (\hat{y}_{ij} - y_{i})^2} \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{y}_{ij} \in \mathbf{\tilde{\mathbf{V}}}_{p,\alpha} \label{eq:loss}\end{equation} where:.
elements.sent-2411-134: The overall architecture of the neural network we used is shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig: NN architecture , which is designed based on our previous work~\cite{xiang2024imitation .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: The input to the neural network consists of sampled points and the desired confidence level.
elements.sent-2411-136: Before sending these inputs to the neural network, we normalize them first.
elements.sent-2411-137: The confidence level and the sample points are then embedded in a higher dimension using a linear layer.
elements.sent-2411-138: The embedded items are concatenated together.
elements.sent-2411-139: These concatenated embeddings are then sent to the transformer layer.
elements.sent-2411-140: In the transformer, the channel representing the confidence level interacts with all the point channels.
elements.sent-2411-141: Finally, we send the confidence level channel to an output layer, resulting in four vertices of the convex hull.
elements.sent-2411-142: Because sample points are drawn from different distributions, they have very different means and variances, which can cause challenges for the training process of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-143: Normalizing those points before feeding them into the neural network is very crucial for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-144: First, if certain inputs have larger values and higher variance than others, they may dominate the training process.

elements.sent-2411-271: Identifying the obstacle space is crucial for path planning.
elements.sent-2411-272: However, generating an accurate obstacle space remains a significant challenge due to various sources of uncertainty, including motion, behavior, and perception limitations.
elements.sent-2411-273: Even though an autonomous system can operate with an inaccurate obstacle space by being over-conservative and using redundant sensors, a more accurate obstacle space generator can reduce both path planning costs and hardware costs.
elements.sent-2411-274: Existing generation methods that generate high-quality output are all computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-275: Traditional methods, such as filtering, sensor fusion and data-driven estimators, face significant computational challenges or require large amounts of data, which limits their applicability in realistic scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-276: In this paper, we propose leveraging neural networks, commonly used in imitation learning, to mimic expert methods for modeling uncertainty and generating confidence regions for obstacle positions, which we refer to as the probabilistic obstacle space.
elements.sent-2411-277: The network is trained using a multi-label, supervised learning approach.
elements.sent-2411-278: We adopt a fine-tuned convex approximation method as the expert to construct training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-279: After training, given only a small number of samples, the neural network can accurately replicate the probabilistic obstacle space while achieving substantially faster generation speed.
elements.sent-2411-280: Moreover, the resulting obstacle space is convex, making it more convenient for subsequent path planning.
elements.sent-2411-281: One of the most important tasks of any autonomous vehicle is to move without collisions, in other words, to remain within a safe space at all times~\cite{dawson2020provably .}

elements.sent-2411-305: In this paper, we propose a supervised learning approach that employs multi-label training to train a transformer-based neural network for generating polygonal probabilistic obstacle spaces under uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-306: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the network can reliably reproduce probabilistic obstacle spaces when the input samples are drawn from obstacle types it has learned.
elements.sent-2411-307: In addition, the proposed method achieves substantially faster generation times compared to baseline approaches.
elements.sent-2411-308: The method offers four key advantages:.
elements.sent-2411-309: Advantage 1: The approach does not rely on dynamic models of uncertain obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-310: Instead, it can construct obstacle spaces from only a few noisy measurements or partial observations, reducing the sensing and modeling requirements.
elements.sent-2411-311: Advantage 2: All generated obstacle spaces are convex and linear, which simplifies their integration into path planning algorithms and ensures computational tractability.
elements.sent-2411-312: Advantage 3: Training requires only a small amount of labeled data, which lowers the burden of data collection and annotation and makes the approach practical for large-scale deployment.
elements.sent-2411-313: Advantage 4: The generation process is extremely fast, enabling real-time use in online planning and decision-making systems.
elements.sent-2411-314: The main goal of this paper is to generate the probabilistic obstacle space of an uncertain obstacle from a limited data sample using a neural network: \begin{equation}\label{eq: nn} o^{\alpha} = NN(w, \mathbf{s}),\end{equation} where $o^{\alpha}$ is the probabilistic obstacle space at confidence level $\alpha \in (0,1)$, $w$ denotes the neural network parameters, and $\mathbf{s = {s^i _{i=1 ^n$ is the set of observed $n$ samples.}}}
elements.sent-2411-316: Let $o^{true}$ be the true occupied region of an uncertain obstacle $o$, and let $\mathbb{P}$ denote the underlying probability measure that characterizes uncertainty in the obstacle’s position.
elements.sent-2411-317: A set $o^{\alpha}$ is called a probabilistic obstacle space at confidence level $\alpha$ if it satisfies \begin{equation}\label{eq: prob_def} \mathbb{P}[a \in o^{true} \mid a \notin o^{\alpha}] < 1 - \alpha.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-318: That is, if the agent $a$ avoids $o^{\alpha}$, then the probability of entering the true obstacle region $o^{true}$ is bounded above by $1 - \alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-319: Since $o^{true}$ is generally unknown due to imperfect perception and prediction, $o^{\alpha}$ provides a tractable confidence-region approximation.
elements.sent-2411-320: Our definition generalizes several prior notions, including the $\epsilon$-shadow of obstacle spaces~\cite{axelrod2018provably, shimanuki2021hardness , Gaussian-distributed obstacles~\cite{wu2020probabilistically , adaptive bounding boxes~\cite{timans2024adaptive , and $\alpha$-probabilistic occupied sets~\cite{9157911 .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-347: This data-driven method builds on a kernel density estimator (KDE) that can generate the probabilistic obstacle space, accelerated by a fast Fourier transform (FFT).
elements.sent-2411-348: These KDE values are then utilized within an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) framework to find a minimal parallelogram box that approximates the probabilistic obstacle space.
elements.sent-2411-349: After obtaining the expert polygon vertices $\tilde{\mathcal{V }$, we prepare the input data for network training.}
elements.sent-2411-350: Fig.~\ref{Fig: maskedExample illustrates how different subsets of samples are presented to the neural network during training.}
elements.sent-2411-351: At each training step, a small random subset of samples is selected, and the transformer is trained to generate the corresponding polygon vertices.
elements.sent-2411-352: Through this supervised learning process, the network is repeatedly exposed to multiple partial views of the same obstacle, learning to associate incomplete sample sets with the full probabilistic obstacle space.
elements.sent-2411-353: At inference time, when only a limited number of samples are available, the trained network can infer the underlying distribution of the obstacle space by leveraging the patterns it has learned from these partial-to-full mappings.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/multi_label_training.png} \caption{Multi-label learning: during training, the network is guided to produce outputs that align with the closest expert solution.} \label{Fig: multi_label_learning}
elements.sent-2411-354: Recall the non-uniqueness of confidence regions~\cite{neyman1937outline : for any given distribution, there exist infinitely many subsets that capture the same probability mass.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Consequently, for each uncertain obstacle, there are infinitely many valid probabilistic obstacle spaces.
elements.sent-2411-356: This motivates the use of multi-label learning rather than single-label learning.
elements.sent-2411-358: Given the current network parameters, the neural network produces an output polygon.
elements.sent-2411-359: The loss is then computed between this output and each available expert probabilistic obstacle space.
elements.sent-2411-360: The smallest loss among them is used to update the network weights.
elements.sent-2411-361: In practice, multiple expert probabilistic obstacle spaces are provided for each obstacle during training.
elements.sent-2411-362: Similar to many regression-based approaches, we employ the mean squared error (MSE) loss, as shown in Eq.~\ref{eq:loss , which minimizes the average discrepancy between the network output and the expert labels. \begin{equation}\text{Loss} = \min_{j \in [1, 10]}{\frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} (\hat{y}_{ij} - y_{i})^2} \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{y}_{ij} \in \mathbf{\tilde{\mathcal{V}}} \label{eq:loss}\end{equation} where:.}
elements.sent-2411-363: Finally, we send the confidence level channel to an output layer, resulting in four vertices of the convex polygon.
elements.sent-2411-364: Because sample points are drawn from different tasks, they have very different means and variances, which can cause challenges for the training process of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-365: First, if some inputs have larger values and higher variance than others, they may dominate the training process.
elements.sent-2411-366: For this work, we only use the transformer encoder~\cite{vaswani2017attention because our task requires predicting all polygon vertices at once, instead of generating them sequentially.}
elements.sent-2411-367: The encoder is composed of a stack of 6 identical layers, each layer has two sub-layers, a multi-head self-attention mechanism, and a feedforward network.
elements.sent-2411-368: In prior masked autoencoder research, mask tokens are introduced after the encoder so that the decoder can identify which missing positions need to be reconstructed.
elements.sent-2411-370: Unlike reconstruction tasks, we do not use mask tokens, since the visible samples do not carry positional information about which part of the obstacle space they come from.
elements.sent-2411-371: Moreover, sequence order is irrelevant in our problem.
elements.sent-2411-372: In natural language processing (NLP), for example, the sentences ‘The dog chased the cat’ and ‘The cat chased the dog’ convey completely different meanings despite containing the same words.
elements.sent-2411-373: In contrast, for our problem, the point sets ${(1,1),(2,2)}$ and ${(2,2),(1,1)}$ represent the same distribution.
elements.sent-2411-374: Consequently, no additional global context is required.
elements.sent-2411-375: Furthermore, the objective of our neural network is not to reconstruct the full underlying distribution but to generate a convex bounding region.
elements.sent-2411-376: Thus, a simple output layer suffices to produce the convex bound from the features extracted by the encoder.

elements.sent-2411-380: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method, we conduct experiments on three types of uncertain obstacles: flying obstacles subject to motion uncertainty, obstacles with uncertain destinations, and obstacles whose positions are estimated using noisy sensor measurements.
elements.sent-2411-381: We feed the neural network with samples drawn from the true obstacle distribution and desired confidence level $\alpha$, then evaluate whether the generated probabilistic obstacle space achieves the desired coverage, i.e., whether it bounds the specified proportion of the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-382: Obstacles of the same type share the same underlying distribution but may exhibit different geometric shapes.
elements.sent-2411-383: We trained a neural network that can work with varying values of $\alpha$, where $\alpha$ can be 80\%, 85\%, 90\%, 95\% and 99\%.
elements.sent-2411-384: To accelerate the training process, we prepare 100 sets of expert vertices for each desired confidence level and prepare 320,000 sets of 100 samples before training began.
elements.sent-2411-385: In each training iteration, we randomly select one set of samples and one of $\alpha$ as the neural network's input, then randomly assign ten sets of corresponding expert vertices as the target.
elements.sent-2411-386: We can prepare the training dataset for a known uncertain obstacle within a few hours.
elements.sent-2411-387: It supports the Advantage 3 of the proposed method, the training dataset is easy to make.
elements.sent-2411-388: We tested the trained neural networks by presenting them with 5,000 random tasks (1000 for each $\alpha$) from each type of uncertain obstacle.
elements.sent-2411-389: Each task contains 100 samples randomly generated from the real distribution of the uncertain obstacle and has never been seen by the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-390: The neural network generates the corresponding $o^{\alpha}$ for those samples.
elements.sent-2411-391: To evaluate the $o^{\alpha}$ generated by the neural network, we first need to determine the coverage.
elements.sent-2411-392: We approximate the true obstacle distribution using 100,000 samples, as the underlying distributions are typically empirical.
elements.sent-2411-393: The resulting coverage was computed as the percentage of samples that fell within the generated $o^{\alpha}$.
elements.sent-2411-394: We use mean squared error (MSE) to quantify the accuracy of the $o^{\alpha}$: \begin{equation}\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left( \alpha_i - \hat{\alpha}_i \right)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-395: While a low MSE indicates that the probabilistic obstacle space is accurate, it does not guarantee correctness.
elements.sent-2411-396: Therefore, it is essential to compare the percentage of instances where $\alpha_i$ exceeds $\hat{\alpha _i$.}
elements.sent-2411-397: We called it the correctness rate: \begin{equation}\text{Correctness Rate} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}\!\left[ \alpha_i > \hat{\alpha}_i \right],\end{equation} where $1[\bullet]$ is the indicator function(1 if the condition is true, 0 otherwise).
elements.sent-2411-398: We compare the proposed method with the ILP Heuristic (ILP), which is the expert method, and the bounding box (BB) method~\cite{zhao2012algorithm}\cite{ericson2004real , a popular approach to finding a polygonal approximation of the obstacle space.}
elements.sent-2411-399: We generate $o^{\alpha}$ with BB and ILP for the same obstacle as the baseline.
elements.sent-2411-400: Fig.~\ref{fig:generating_demo are examples of obstacle distribution and $o^{\alpha}$ generated by different methods given the same samples.}
elements.sent-2411-401: However, since BB and ILP are not learning-based methods and do not require a training process, a direct comparison may not be entirely fair.
elements.sent-2411-402: To address this, we also provide the baseline methods with 20 times more samples to enable a more thorough evaluation of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-403: In addition to these baselines, we implement the MINLP Optimal Algorithm~\cite{wu2024data2 to compute the smallest convex region that satisfies the coverage requirement.}
elements.sent-2411-404: For motion uncertainty, we evaluate flying obstacles subject to stochastic deviations in their trajectories.

elements.sent-2411-429: Even with 20$\times$ more samples, BB and ILP fail to match NN’s accuracy and stability.
elements.sent-2411-430: Correctness rates remain above 98\% for NN, ensuring that the generated obstacle spaces are reliable.
elements.sent-2411-431: Moreover, the obstacle space areas are significantly closer to the optimal solution compared with BB and ILP.
elements.sent-2411-432: This result keeps demonstrating NN’s ability to generalize well under noisy measurement conditions while avoiding excessive conservatism.
elements.sent-2411-433: Across motion, behavior, and sensor uncertainty, the proposed NN consistently outperforms traditional BB and ILP methods.
elements.sent-2411-434: Three key observations can be drawn:.
elements.sent-2411-435: Accuracy with Few Samples: NN achieves high accuracy and low MSE even with very limited data, whereas baselines require 20$\times$ more samples to approach similar performance.
elements.sent-2411-436: Reliability: NN maintains correctness rates above 93\% in all scenarios, ensuring that the generated probabilistic obstacle spaces meet or exceed the desired confidence levels.
elements.sent-2411-437: Efficiency: NN produces compact obstacle spaces that are significantly smaller than BB while avoiding the instability of ILP.
elements.sent-2411-438: This efficiency is particularly critical in path planning applications where over-approximation reduces safe space.
elements.sent-2411-439: These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed learning-based framework in generating accurate and reliable probabilistic obstacle spaces under diverse forms of uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-440: Generating time is crucial in deciding whether a method can be used in online applications.
elements.sent-2411-441: Therefore, we compare the time to generate one correct probabilistic obstacle space by each method to prove our method is more useful in online applications.
elements.sent-2411-442: The result supports that the Advantage 4 of the proposed method, NN can generate a useful probabilistic obstacle space very fast.
elements.sent-2411-443: In this paper, we propose using supervised learning to train a transformer-based neural network to generate a confidence region of the real obstacle space distribution, which we define as the probabilistic obstacle space.
elements.sent-2411-444: Once trained, the neural network can robustly generate vertices of the polygon that cover the desired percentage of the real obstacle region, only given a few samples.
elements.sent-2411-445: No dynamic information about the obstacle such as disturbance is required.
